2026-04-16 20:48 · 9,142 stocks · 186 API calls

🤖 AI Top Picks — Claude Sonnet
1. DOCS Doximity, Inc. · Healthcare HIGH ACT NOW Quality Compounder

"LinkedIn for doctors — 80% of US physicians on one platform — trading like an also-ran at 32% of its 52-week high"

The market is treating DOCS as a cyclical pharma-ad play that got hit when drug companies cut marketing budgets, but the underlying asset is a near-monopoly professional network with 80%+ physician penetration that competitors simply cannot replicate — Veeva and Epic lack the social layer, LinkedIn lacks clinical workflow trust, and starting over would take a decade. Revenue growth just RE-ACCELERATED from 13% to 20%, FCF margin is 49%, ROIC is 20%, the balance sheet holds 20% of market cap in net cash with zero debt, and the stock sits at 32% of its 52-week high. The market is pricing peak-pharma-ad-spend-fear as a permanent condition on a platform with structural, not cyclical, revenue.

Price $24.38 PEG 1.6 P/E 18.7 ROIC 20.5% Catalyst Q1 FY2027 earnings in approximately 6 weeks — if pharma ad spend recovery is confirmed and full-year guidance is raised, the de-rating from 52-week highs fully reverses; consensus has set a low bar. Watch A major pharma consolidation wave (e.g. post-M&A budget freezes) or CMS-level changes to pharmaceutical marketing regulations could structurally reduce the addressable ad spend pool, which is the one exogenous risk this thesis cannot control.
2. DUOL Duolingo, Inc. · Technology HIGH ACT NOW Turnaround

"500 million users, 39% revenue growth, GAAP profitable — sitting at 19% of its 52-week high because Wall Street still thinks it's a free app"

Duolingo crossed into sustained GAAP profitability in 2025 (PE=11.1 trailing) but the market is anchored to its prior loss-making identity — when a company goes from burning cash to 36% FCF margins while growing at 39%, the re-rating lag is an opportunity, not a warning. At PEG 0.7 with 22% of market cap in net cash and 74% DCF margin of safety, this is the cheapest consumer subscription compounder in the market relative to growth, yet analysts are still writing it up as 'edtech' rather than 'the Spotify of language learning.' The AI-powered subscription tier expansion (Super and Max) is still in early monetization innings with only a fraction of 500M+ users paying.

Price $103.32 PEG 0.7 P/E 11.1 ROIC 9.4% Catalyst Q1 2026 earnings in approximately 4 weeks — AI subscription tier (Max) paid conversion data and subscriber growth acceleration should force consensus EPS estimates sharply higher, catalyzing a re-rating from 'app company' to 'subscription compounder.' Watch Google or Apple launching a deeply integrated, free AI language tutor natively on their platforms remains the single existential competitive risk — Duolingo's moat is behavioral, not technological, and a device-level competitor with zero marginal cost is the bear case.
3. PEGA Pegasystems Inc. · Technology HIGH WITHIN MONTHS Quality Compounder

"Enterprise AI workflow software with 29% ROIC growing at 17%, trading at PEG 0.8 — half the price it deserves"

Pega is an enterprise software company that automates the most complex, mission-critical workflows — insurance claims, banking compliance, telecom provisioning — and once embedded, it is virtually impossible to replace without a multi-year rip-and-replace project costing tens of millions. Revenue just re-accelerated from 5% to 17% as the shift to cloud ACV bookings converts to recognized revenue, but the PEG sits at 0.8 because the market is still anchored to the slow-growth narrative from 2022-2023. ROIC of 29%, FCF conversion of 1.2x, and 76% gross margins confirm the earnings quality is genuine — this is not financial engineering.

Price $43.37 PEG 0.8 P/E 18.5 ROIC 28.9% Catalyst Q2 2026 earnings in approximately 3 months — confirmation that cloud ARR backlog conversion is accelerating and operating margins are expanding as cloud mix grows should force a re-rating from PE 18.5 to sector-appropriate 25-30x given the growth profile. Watch ServiceNow or Salesforce aggressively bundling AI workflow tools at discounted prices within existing enterprise contracts is the most credible competitive displacement risk — Pega's smaller scale limits its ability to match pricing wars with $200B+ incumbents.
4. QLYS Qualys, Inc. · Technology HIGH WITHIN MONTHS IV Discount

"Cybersecurity royalty machine — 46% FCF margin, 28% ROIC, zero leverage — trading at PE 15x like it's a struggling mid-cap"

Qualys has quietly become one of the most capital-efficient businesses in cybersecurity: it earns 28% ROIC, converts 46% of revenue to free cash, holds net cash equal to 12% of its market cap, and has not missed a revenue target in years — yet trades at PE 15.4x, below its own sector average PEG of 2.2, and 54% of its 52-week high. The market is pricing it as a slow-growth commodity scanning tool, missing that its TruRisk platform and QMDR managed detection product are opening enterprise upsell cycles that will show up in NRR expansion. A 74% DCF margin of safety on a business with zero debt and royalty-like cash flows is an institutional-grade anomaly.

Price $83.23 PEG 1.6 P/E 15.4 ROIC 28.1% Catalyst Q2 2026 earnings in approximately 3 months showing enterprise seat expansion re-accelerating via TruRisk upsell data — if NRR moves above 110%, the 'slow growth' narrative collapses and re-rating to 20-22x earnings is the base case. Watch Microsoft Security's deepening bundled offering (Defender for Cloud, Sentinel) at near-zero incremental cost for existing Azure customers is the most credible displacement risk — if Microsoft bundles vulnerability management into E5 licenses more aggressively, Qualys's renewal cycle faces pricing pressure.
5. DLO DLocal Limited · Technology HIGH WITHIN MONTHS Fast Grower

"The payment rails for 40 emerging markets — Netflix and Amazon depend on it — growing 47% a year at PEG 0.9"

DLocal is the infrastructure layer that allows global merchants like Netflix, Amazon, Uber, and Spotify to collect payments from consumers in Brazil, Mexico, Nigeria, India, and 40+ other markets where local payment methods (Pix, UPI, mobile money, local cards) are the only way to transact. Building this in each country requires regulatory licenses, banking relationships, and local compliance expertise that took DLocal years to assemble — a well-funded competitor starting today would need 5+ years to replicate at meaningful scale. Yet the stock trades at PEG 0.9 with 29% ROIC, 37% FCF margins, and 19% net cash — the discount exists purely because 'Uruguay-domiciled EM payments' sounds exotic to US fund managers.

Price $13.60 PEG 0.9 P/E 20.3 ROIC 28.5% Catalyst Next quarterly earnings confirming take-rate stabilization and continued Total Payment Volume acceleration across new corridors — specifically African market expansion traction — should force the market to price this as the EM infrastructure compounder it is rather than a speculative EM fintech. Watch Brazilian regulatory changes to Pix instant payment infrastructure or a local competitor gaining a major exclusive with a tier-1 merchant would be the most damaging single-market shock; Brazil represents a disproportionate share of DLO's revenue, making concentration risk the thesis's primary vulnerability.
6. ADBE Adobe Inc. · Technology HIGH WITHIN WEEKS Quality Compounder

"37% ROIC, 42% FCF margin, Adobe Creative Cloud owns creative professionals worldwide — at PE 13.9x because AI fear has created a gift"

Adobe is the defining example of a market mispricing AI as a threat to a business that is actually becoming more valuable through AI — Firefly AI is adding new paid tiers, ARPU is expanding, and the creative workflow integration runs so deep (Photoshop, Premiere, Acrobat, Figma tools) that switching to an 'AI alternative' means abandoning the entire professional workflow. The stock sits at 59% of its 52-week high with a PE of 13.9x and a 28% DCF margin of safety — this is a $101B company with 89% gross margins, 37% ROIC, and 42% FCF margins trading at less than a basic consumer staple multiple. The AI-disruption narrative is the mispricing, not the reality.

Price $247.84 PEG 1.5 P/E 13.9 ROIC 37.4% Catalyst Q2 FY2026 earnings in approximately 6 weeks or Adobe MAX conference validating that Firefly AI monetization through new paid tiers is adding measurably to Creative Cloud ARPU — this one data point resolves the AI-disruption overhang that has compressed the multiple for 18 months. Watch If Firefly ARPU data at earnings is ambiguous or management guides conservatively on AI monetization, the re-rating thesis loses its near-term catalyst and the stock remains range-bound until FY2027 evidence accumulates — patience required if earnings disappoint.
7. MNDY monday.com Ltd. · Technology MEDIUM WITHIN WEEKS Turnaround

"Work operating system turning profitable — fwd PE 8.2x vs trailing 28.5x, 40% of market cap in net cash, 27% revenue growth"

Monday.com just crossed into GAAP profitability and the market is completely anchored to its prior loss-making identity — the forward PE of 8.2x versus trailing 28.5x is not a data error, it is an earnings inflection that analysts haven't repriced yet. The company holds 40% of market cap in net cash, grows at 27%, earns 89% gross margins, and FCF conversion is 2.7x — this is a business generating real cash, not burning it. The 'work OS' positioning (project management + CRM + service management) is expanding wallet share per enterprise customer, and the near-zero trailing operating margin is in the rearview mirror.

Price $65.07 PEG 1.6 P/E 28.5 ROIC -0.1% Catalyst Q1 2026 earnings in approximately 4 weeks — if enterprise module adoption data (CRM, service management) confirms that operating margin expansion is ahead of schedule and FCF conversion remains above 2x, the market's anchoring to the trailing PE collapses and re-rating is rapid. Watch Revenue growth decelerated from 33% to 27% and any further slowdown at the earnings print would reignite the 'decelerating growth platform' narrative; the 40% net cash provides a floor but a miss on enterprise expansion metrics would delay re-rating by 2-3 quarters.
8. HALO Halozyme Therapeutics, Inc. · Healthcare MEDIUM WITHIN MONTHS IV Discount

"Biotech royalty platform — not a drug company — with 58% operating margins and fwd PE 7.6x being mispriced as development-stage risk"

The market classifies Halozyme as a biotech and prices it with biotech-style optionality risk, when in reality it is a royalty and technology licensing platform — its ENHANZE drug delivery technology is licensed to Roche, J&J, AstraZeneca, and others to convert IV drugs to subcutaneous administration, collecting royalties on every dose sold. With 58% operating margins, 82% gross margins, 24% ROIC, and a forward PE of 7.6x versus trailing 25.3x, the earnings inflection from new partner launches is clearly not priced in. There is no pipeline risk here — ENHANZE is already proven and the royalty stream grows as existing partners expand their labeled indications.

Price $67.09 PEG 0.7 P/E 25.3 ROIC 23.6% Catalyst New partner launches converting to royalty revenue in H2 2026 — specifically J&J's subcutaneous formulations and upcoming partner pipeline readouts — should demonstrate that forward earnings estimates are conservative and the fwd PE 7.6x is an anomaly. Watch EG5y estimate is only +2% despite current 38% revenue growth, suggesting analysts are skeptical of royalty stream duration — if a key partner's drug faces patent expiry or market access challenges earlier than modeled, the royalty base shrinks faster than priced.

⚠ Key Risks

  • DUOL and MNDY: if Q1 2026 earnings (4 weeks out) disappoint on subscriber growth or FCF conversion, the 19-21% of 52-week high entry becomes a value trap — watch DAU and subscription ARPU closely
  • DLO: emerging-market FX volatility and any single-country regulatory action (Brazil, Nigeria) could compress take-rates sharply — the 47% revenue growth is corridor-concentration risk in disguise
  • QLYS and PEGA: enterprise software budget freezes in a slowdown scenario hit renewal cycles 6-9 months before they show in revenue — monitor NRR and ARR backlog at Q2 2026 earnings

ELEVATED

VIX at 18.4 reflects underlying nervousness — not panic, but not complacency — while the 10Y at 4.26% sustains valuation pressure on expensive growth names; the combination of above-target CPI and Fed on hold creates a grinding, stock-picking environment where quality FCF generators outperform index-level exposure, but any CPI resurgence or labor market deterioration could snap sentiment quickly.

AI MARKET SYNOPSIS

The market is mislabeling durable software and healthcare compounders as speculative growth names simply because rates are elevated and sentiment is cautious — yet these businesses generate 28-51% ROIC with 36-49% FCF margins, meaning they fund their own growth without needing cheap capital. The real opportunity is in the multi-specialist consensus names where Wall Street's macro fear has created entry points that would normally require a recession to achieve.

🌍 GEOPOLITICAL & MACRO CONTEXT

The 10Y at 4.26% with a normal yield curve (+53bps) and VIX at 18.4 signals a cautious but not panicked market — elevated rates compress pure-growth multiples but reward FCF generators, making 40-49% FCF margin names like DOCS, QLYS, and DUOL the right defensive-growth posture. CPI at 3.29% above target keeps the Fed from cutting aggressively (funds rate 3.64%), sustaining the duration headwind on long-dated growth stories, but businesses with near-term earnings catalysts and sub-15x trailing PE are insulated from that compression. Unemployment at 4.3% is healthy — no recession signal yet — which supports consumer-facing and enterprise IT spend continuing through 2026.
